  3. Decision trees

Decision trees

CSS architecture decision trees

Use these when CSS arguments start, code reviews lose clarity, or someone reaches for !important before asking where the code belongs.

Start first Utility or component Modifier or state Base, theme, or component Hack or real fix Layout or component Scope, BEM, or neither

Ask this first

Stop adding specificity first.

Ask: Am I changing behaviour, presentation, structure, or temporary state? That answer decides the destination before writing CSS.

Most bad CSS starts with the wrong destination, not the wrong selector. Decide ownership first.

Utility or component?

Modifier or state?

Base vs theme vs component

Hack or real fix?

Layout problem or component problem?

@scope, BEM, or neither?

Next useful pages

Layers

Control precedence before increasing specificity.

Starter template

See where these decisions live inside a real project structure.

Anti-patterns

Recognise the mistakes these decision trees are trying to prevent.

LSCSS

Layered Semantic CSS. A practical methodology for calm, maintainable front-end architecture.

Evolves with real delivery. Change what does not fit your codebase.
